1	# Fixture: volcano

3	Volcano plot for ~3000 genes. Highlight up/down/non-sig with thresholds in data.json.
4	## Inputs
5	- data.json: {log2fc, neg_log10_p, thresholds}
6	## Outputs
7	- figure.pdf, figure.png; caption.tex; gen_figure.py

10	- Optional: annotate top-5 by neg_log10_p magnitude.

# Fixture: volcano
## Task
Volcano plot for ~3000 genes. Highlight up/down/non-sig with thresholds in data.json.
## Inputs
- data.json: {log2fc, neg_log10_p, thresholds}
## Outputs
- figure.pdf, figure.png; caption.tex; gen_figure.py
## Hints
- 3 colors: down (left), non-sig (center), up (right).
- Optional: annotate top-5 by neg_log10_p magnitude.

content.content:
1	---
2	slug: academic-plotting
3	summary: Publication-quality figure standards — rcParams + PALETTE-dict pattern + outside ticks for content discipline; matplotlib for numerical axes, diagram tools for structure; vector + raster outputs reproducibly from a checked-in script. Layout discipline lives in figure-layout-defaults.
4	layer: logical
5	tools:
6	version: 3
7	status: active
8	supersedes:
9	references: figure-spec, interactive-figure-prototype, figure-layout-defaults, pdf-vector-layout
10	provenance: human + SkillTest-R1.A+R1.B-merged
11	---
12	
13	# Skill — Academic Plotting
14	
15	Two responsibilities: pick the right tool for the figure shape, and apply venue-grade content discipline. Layout (gridspec, panel hierarchy, no-empty-quadrants) lives in `figure-layout-defaults`.
16	
17	## When to invoke
18	
19	- Before every new figure goes into `branches/writer/paper/figures/`.
20	- When polishing a figure Coder produced — improve readability without changing scientific meaning.
21	- Before camera-ready, audit every figure against this checklist.
22	
23	## Tool by figure shape
24	
25	| Figure shape | Tool |
26	|---|---|
27	| Numerical axes (bars, lines, scatter, heatmap, violin) | matplotlib / seaborn |
28	| Boxes-and-arrows (architecture, pipeline, workflow, cascade) | diagram tool — see `figure-spec` |
29	
30	Chart-type-from-data-shape: time / step on x → line; N methods × M benchmarks → grouped bar; single ranking → horizontal bar; two continuous vars → scatter; square matrix → heatmap; proportions → stacked bar (avoid pie in ML papers).
31	
32	## Content discipline (apply to every script)
33	
34	### 1. rcParams block at the top
35	
36	Every plotting script for a paper figure leads with:
37	
38	```python
39	import matplotlib as mpl
40	mpl.rcParams.update({
41	    "font.family": "sans-serif",
42	    "font.sans-serif": ["Arial", "Helvetica", "DejaVu Sans", "Liberation Sans"],
43	    "svg.fonttype": "none",     # editable text in SVG
44	    "pdf.fonttype": 42,         # editable TrueType in PDF
45	    "axes.spines.right": False,
46	    "axes.spines.top": False,
47	    "axes.linewidth": 0.8,
48	    "legend.frameon": False,
49	})
50	```
51	
52	Editable text in vector outputs is a HARD Nature-family submission requirement. Verify with `grep -c '<text' fig.svg` — 0 means rasterised paths, the figure will be flagged.
53	
54	**Font unification discipline (R-future-4):** the font stack is set ONCE in this rcParams block and NEVER overridden per element. Every `ax.text`, `ax.annotate`, `ax.set_xlabel`, `ax.set_title`, and `fig.text` call inherits from rcParams — no `fontfamily=`, `fontname=`, or `font=` kwargs anywhere else in the script. This ensures cross-version consistency: if the figure is regenerated by different people or across sessions, the rcParams block is the single source of truth. Pin it in a comment:
55	
56	```python
57	# Font stack: Arial → Helvetica → DejaVu Sans → Liberation Sans
58	# Do NOT override per element — all text inherits from here.
59	```
60	
61	### 2. PALETTE-dict pattern, single dict per script
62	
63	```python
64	PALETTE = {
65	    "signal": "#0F4D92",
66	    "signal_soft": "#B4C0E4",
67	    "neutral": "#767676",
68	    "neutral_light": "#D8D8D8",
69	    "accent": "#E4CCD8",
70	    "accent_dark": "#9A4D8E",
71	    "black": "#272727",
72	}
73	```
74	
75	Discipline rule: **green and red are for directional signals only** (gain / loss / up / down / KO-vs-WT). Categorical labels (cluster IDs, condition names without direction) use neutrals — grey + single-blue family + faint pink. A saturated 3-or-4 hue cycle on a cluster sidebar burns the green/red pair on labels that carry no direction.
76	
77	Default palette: Okabe-Ito `#E69F00 #56B4E9 #009E73 #F0E442 #0072B2 #D55E00 #CC79A7` is colorblind-safe and remains the right starting point when the figure has no directional split.
78	
79	### 3. Figure size in inches, not millimetres
80	
81	| Figure | Default figsize (inches) |
82	|---|---|
83	| Single panel | `(6, 4)` |
84	| 2-panel side-by-side | `(10, 4)` |
85	| 4-panel hero | `(11, 6)` (see `figure-layout-defaults`) |
86	| Single-column publication | `~3.3"` width |
87	| Full-width publication | `~7"` width |
88	
89	Compress to exact mm only at submission packaging stage, AFTER layout is visually confirmed. Leading with `figsize=(width_mm/25.4, height_mm/25.4)` produces unreadable cramming.
90	
91	### 4. TwoSlopeNorm for diverging colormaps
92	
93	```python
94	norm = mpl.colors.TwoSlopeNorm(vmin=-vmax, vcenter=0, vmax=vmax)
95	im = ax.imshow(data, cmap="RdBu_r", norm=norm)
96	```
97	
98	The zero line on a z-score or log-fold-change heatmap is load-bearing. Do not rely on data-symmetric `vmin/vmax`.
99	
100	### 5. Outside ticks
101	
102	```python
103	ax.tick_params(direction="out", length=2.2, width=0.6)
104	```
105	
106	Inside ticks fight the data; outside reads as instrument-style chrome.
107	
108	### 6. Labels and weights
109	
110	Axis label 9–10 pt; tick label 8 pt; line width ≥ 1.5 pt; marker size ≥ 5 pt. Reserve one distinct accent color for "our method" / the directional signal of interest.
111	
112	## Reproducibility
113	
114	Plotting scripts at `branches/writer/paper/figures/gen_fig_<name>.py` read concrete data files from `branches/coder/exp/` or `branches/shared/exp/exp-<id>/`. No hardcoded numbers. Re-run reproduces byte-identical output modulo font rendering.
115	
116	Export both formats: `fig.savefig(path.pdf)` for LaTeX inclusion, `fig.savefig(path.png, dpi=300)` for slides / web. Verify LaTeX includes the PDF without font warnings; verify the SVG has non-zero `<text>` nodes.
117	
118	Each figure ships as: `gen_fig_<name>.py`, `fig_<name>.pdf`, `fig_<name>.png`, plus a one-line provenance docstring citing the source data file.
119	
120	## Pitfalls
121	
122	- 0 `<text>` nodes in saved SVG — copy-editors cannot edit, venue will flag.
123	- Saturated 3-or-4 hue cycle on a categorical cluster bar — burns green/red on direction-less labels.
124	- Hardcoding numbers into plotting scripts — breaks reproducibility and evidence trace.
125	- Over-decorating: gradients, 3D bars, drop shadows. Reviewer distrust ("if the figure is decorated, what is being hidden?") and venue raster-reproducibility issues.
126	- Tiny axis labels unreadable at column width.
127	- Mm-first figsize — drives cramming. Mm conversion is a packaging move.
128	- Inside ticks fighting the data.
129	- Diverging cmap without TwoSlopeNorm — zero line drifts off centre by data luck.
130

file.content:
---
slug: academic-plotting
summary: Publication-quality figure standards — rcParams + PALETTE-dict pattern + outside ticks for content discipline; matplotlib for numerical axes, diagram tools for structure; vector + raster outputs reproducibly from a checked-in script. Layout discipline lives in figure-layout-defaults.
layer: logical
tools:
version: 3
status: active
supersedes:
references: figure-spec, interactive-figure-prototype, figure-layout-defaults, pdf-vector-layout
provenance: human + SkillTest-R1.A+R1.B-merged
---

# Skill — Academic Plotting

Two responsibilities: pick the right tool for the figure shape, and apply venue-grade content discipline. Layout (gridspec, panel hierarchy, no-empty-quadrants) lives in `figure-layout-defaults`.

## When to invoke

- Before every new figure goes into `branches/writer/paper/figures/`.
- When polishing a figure Coder produced — improve readability without changing scientific meaning.
- Before camera-ready, audit every figure against this checklist.

## Tool by figure shape

| Figure shape | Tool |
|---|---|
| Numerical axes (bars, lines, scatter, heatmap, violin) | matplotlib / seaborn |
| Boxes-and-arrows (architecture, pipeline, workflow, cascade) | diagram tool — see `figure-spec` |

Chart-type-from-data-shape: time / step on x → line; N methods × M benchmarks → grouped bar; single ranking → horizontal bar; two continuous vars → scatter; square matrix → heatmap; proportions → stacked bar (avoid pie in ML papers).

## Content discipline (apply to every script)

### 1. rcParams block at the top

Every plotting script for a paper figure leads with:

```python
import matplotlib as mpl
mpl.rcParams.update({
    "font.family": "sans-serif",
    "font.sans-serif": ["Arial", "Helvetica", "DejaVu Sans", "Liberation Sans"],
    "svg.fonttype": "none",     # editable text in SVG
    "pdf.fonttype": 42,         # editable TrueType in PDF
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.linewidth": 0.8,
    "legend.frameon": False,
})
```

Editable text in vector outputs is a HARD Nature-family submission requirement. Verify with `grep -c '<text' fig.svg` — 0 means rasterised paths, the figure will be flagged.

**Font unification discipline (R-future-4):** the font stack is set ONCE in this rcParams block and NEVER overridden per element. Every `ax.text`, `ax.annotate`, `ax.set_xlabel`, `ax.set_title`, and `fig.text` call inherits from rcParams — no `fontfamily=`, `fontname=`, or `font=` kwargs anywhere else in the script. This ensures cross-version consistency: if the figure is regenerated by different people or across sessions, the rcParams block is the single source of truth. Pin it in a comment:

```python
# Font stack: Arial → Helvetica → DejaVu Sans → Liberation Sans
# Do NOT override per element — all text inherits from here.
```

### 2. PALETTE-dict pattern, single dict per script

```python
PALETTE = {
    "signal": "#0F4D92",
    "signal_soft": "#B4C0E4",
    "neutral": "#767676",
    "neutral_light": "#D8D8D8",
    "accent": "#E4CCD8",
    "accent_dark": "#9A4D8E",
    "black": "#272727",
}
```

Discipline rule: **green and red are for directional signals only** (gain / loss / up / down / KO-vs-WT). Categorical labels (cluster IDs, condition names without direction) use neutrals — grey + single-blue family + faint pink. A saturated 3-or-4 hue cycle on a cluster sidebar burns the green/red pair on labels that carry no direction.

Default palette: Okabe-Ito `#E69F00 #56B4E9 #009E73 #F0E442 #0072B2 #D55E00 #CC79A7` is colorblind-safe and remains the right starting point when the figure has no directional split.

### 3. Figure size in inches, not millimetres

| Figure | Default figsize (inches) |
|---|---|
| Single panel | `(6, 4)` |
| 2-panel side-by-side | `(10, 4)` |
| 4-panel hero | `(11, 6)` (see `figure-layout-defaults`) |
| Single-column publication | `~3.3"` width |
| Full-width publication | `~7"` width |

Compress to exact mm only at submission packaging stage, AFTER layout is visually confirmed. Leading with `figsize=(width_mm/25.4, height_mm/25.4)` produces unreadable cramming.

### 4. TwoSlopeNorm for diverging colormaps

```python
norm = mpl.colors.TwoSlopeNorm(vmin=-vmax, vcenter=0, vmax=vmax)
im = ax.imshow(data, cmap="RdBu_r", norm=norm)
```

The zero line on a z-score or log-fold-change heatmap is load-bearing. Do not rely on data-symmetric `vmin/vmax`.

### 5. Outside ticks

```python
ax.tick_params(direction="out", length=2.2, width=0.6)
```

Inside ticks fight the data; outside reads as instrument-style chrome.

### 6. Labels and weights

Axis label 9–10 pt; tick label 8 pt; line width ≥ 1.5 pt; marker size ≥ 5 pt. Reserve one distinct accent color for "our method" / the directional signal of interest.

## Reproducibility

Plotting scripts at `branches/writer/paper/figures/gen_fig_<name>.py` read concrete data files from `branches/coder/exp/` or `branches/shared/exp/exp-<id>/`. No hardcoded numbers. Re-run reproduces byte-identical output modulo font rendering.

Export both formats: `fig.savefig(path.pdf)` for LaTeX inclusion, `fig.savefig(path.png, dpi=300)` for slides / web. Verify LaTeX includes the PDF without font warnings; verify the SVG has non-zero `<text>` nodes.

Each figure ships as: `gen_fig_<name>.py`, `fig_<name>.pdf`, `fig_<name>.png`, plus a one-line provenance docstring citing the source data file.

## Pitfalls

- 0 `<text>` nodes in saved SVG — copy-editors cannot edit, venue will flag.
- Saturated 3-or-4 hue cycle on a categorical cluster bar — burns green/red on direction-less labels.
- Hardcoding numbers into plotting scripts — breaks reproducibility and evidence trace.
- Over-decorating: gradients, 3D bars, drop shadows. Reviewer distrust ("if the figure is decorated, what is being hidden?") and venue raster-reproducibility issues.
- Tiny axis labels unreadable at column width.
- Mm-first figsize — drives cramming. Mm conversion is a packaging move.
- Inside ticks fighting the data.
- Diverging cmap without TwoSlopeNorm — zero line drifts off centre by data luck.

content.content:
1	---
2	slug: figure-aesthetic-exemplars
3	summary: Reference-driven figure design — diff your figure against the closest exemplar in the gallery, identify 3 biggest deltas, revise. Different paradigm from rule-based figure-layout-defaults / academic-plotting.
4	layer: logical
5	tools:
6	version: 1
7	status: draft-from-SkillTest-R-future
8	supersedes:
9	references: figure-layout-defaults, academic-plotting
10	provenance: SkillTest-R6.A-aesthetic-ceiling-finding
11	---
12	
13	# Skill — Figure Aesthetic Exemplars (DRAFT — SkillTest research zone)
14	
15	> This is a draft from SkillTest, not a live MinionsOS skill. Authored
16	> in response to R6.A user feedback: "the skill's palette is maybe slightly
17	> more disciplined, but it's nowhere near beautiful or good-looking."
18	
19	## What this skill is
20	
21	A REFERENCE LIBRARY plus a JUDGEMENT WORKFLOW, not a rule list. The
22	companion skills [[figure-layout-defaults]] and [[academic-plotting]]
23	make figures *correct* (no rasterised text, no clashing hues, no empty
24	quadrants). This skill makes figures *beautiful* — closer to what
25	appears in a recent Nature or Cell publication.
26	
27	The premise: rules can constrain (don't do X) but cannot generate
28	(produce Y). Beauty in scientific figures comes from specific,
29	non-rule-encodable choices: a particular palette ratio, a particular
30	typographic rhythm, a particular allocation of white space. The only
31	way to learn these is to STUDY EXEMPLARS — figures that are
32	empirically beautiful — and DIFF YOUR FIGURE AGAINST THEM.
33	
34	## When to invoke
35	
36	- You have a draft figure that satisfies all rule-based skills
37	  (figure-layout-defaults, academic-plotting) but the user / co-author
38	  has flagged it as "looks fine but not beautiful," "lacks any spark,"
39	  or "nowhere near beautiful or good-looking."
40	- You are preparing a Nature / Cell / Science cover-eligible figure
41	  where aesthetic quality matters beyond submittability.
42	- You have iteration budget: this skill expects 2-4 render-revise cycles,
43	  not single-pass rule application.
44	
45	Do NOT invoke if:
46	- The figure is pre-submission and only needs to be SUBMITTABLE (use
47	  rule-based skills only).
48	- You have no iteration budget — exemplar diffing assumes you can
49	  re-render after revision.
50	
51	## Procedure
52	
53	### Step 1 — Render your figure with rule-based skills first
54	
55	Apply [[figure-layout-defaults]] and [[academic-plotting]] FIRST.
56	Render the figure. Save the artefact at, say, `figures/draft1.png`.
57	Don't skip this — exemplar workflow assumes a baseline-correct figure
58	to refine.
59	
60	### Step 2 — Pick the closest exemplar from the gallery
61	
62	Open `gallery/index.md`. Find the exemplar that matches your figure's
63	ARCHETYPE most closely:
64	
65	| Archetype | Exemplar |
66	|---|---|
67	| Multi-panel hero with dose-response + safety + biomarker | `fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich` |
68	| Heatmap-heavy single-cell / dimension reduction | `fig4-single-cell-systems-rich` |
69	| Single-panel grouped bar (ablation, comparison) | `bars_ablation_Cancer` |
70	| Multi-bar cross-category comparison | `correctness_by_category` |
71	| Multi-method sweep with parameter ranges | `results_sweep` |
72	
73	If your figure type isn't in the gallery, pick the *closest* archetype
74	and note the gap. Future R rounds extend the gallery.
75	
76	### Step 3 — Open the exemplar's annotation card
77	
78	Each exemplar in `gallery/` has a sibling `<exemplar>.annotation.md`
79	file documenting:
80	
81	- **Extracted palette**: actual hex values dominant in the rendered
82	  figure (not generic rules, but what THIS exemplar uses)
83	- **Palette ratio**: percentage of pixels in each colour family
84	  (background grey vs signal accent vs subordinate hue)
85	- **Typography**: font sizes per element class (axis label, panel
86	  letter, caption note, in-panel annotation)
87	- **White space allocation**: ratio of panel area to whitespace
88	- **Visual rhythm**: where the eye lands first, second, third
89	- **What makes this figure work**: 3-5 bullet points naming the
90	  specific design choices that differentiate it from a "correct but
91	  bland" figure
92	
93	### Step 4 — Diff your figure against the exemplar
94	
95	Open both side-by-side (Playwright HTML page or local image viewer).
96	Identify the 3 biggest visual deltas. Examples:
97	
98	- Your palette has 4 saturated hues; exemplar has 1 signal + 2 neutrals
99	  + heavy background grey.
100	- Your panel labels are 7-pt bold; exemplar uses 9-pt bold with extra
101	  weight contrast.
102	- Your hero panel fills 30% of canvas; exemplar's hero fills 45%.
103	- Your bars use 0-100 axis with 35% empty top; exemplar zooms to data
104	  range with 10% headroom.
105	
106	Rank the 3 deltas by aesthetic impact (typography is usually high
107	impact; precise hex value is usually low).
108	
109	### Step 5 — Revise the script to address top-3 deltas
110	
111	DON'T re-do the figure from scratch. Edit the existing script to
112	address only the 3 named deltas. Re-render to `draft2.png`.
113	
114	### Step 6 — Re-diff and decide
115	
116	Compare draft2 vs the exemplar again. If the gap has closed
117	substantially, stop. If 1-2 of the 3 deltas remain, do one more
118	iteration. **Cap at 4 iterations** — beyond that, you're polishing
119	what should have been redesigned.
120	
121	## What this skill does NOT do
122	
123	- It does NOT rewrite [[figure-layout-defaults]] or [[academic-plotting]].
124	  Use those first; this skill is the polish step.
125	- It does NOT generate exemplars. The gallery is curated from real
126	  published figures.
127	- It does NOT replace human aesthetic judgment for a venue cover. For
128	  Nature cover-grade figures, a human designer still adds ~20% beyond
129	  what this workflow produces.
130	- It does NOT tell you a specific colour to use. It tells you ratios
131	  and exemplar precedents; the runner picks specific hex values
132	  matching the manuscript palette family.
133	
134	## Pitfalls
135	
136	- **Picking the wrong exemplar.** If your figure is a single-panel
137	  bar chart and you diff against `fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich` (a
138	  4-panel hero composite), the deltas will be misleading. Match
139	  archetype first.
140	- **Copying the exemplar's palette pixel-perfect.** The point is to
141	  learn the RATIO and the FAMILY, not to literally copy hex values
142	  from a different paper's figure. Translate the lesson to your
143	  manuscript's palette.
144	- **Endless iteration.** 4-iteration cap is a hard limit. If you can't
145	  close the gap in 4 cycles, the figure needs structural redesign,
146	  not aesthetic polish.
147	- **Ignoring the rule-based baseline.** This skill ASSUMES rules are
148	  already applied. If you skip [[figure-layout-defaults]] first, the
149	  exemplar diff becomes "your figure is wrong AND ugly" — fix wrong
150	  first.
151	
152	## Output habit
153	
154	When delivering a polished figure, document:
155	
156	```
157	# aesthetic exemplar workflow
158	exemplar_used: gallery/fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich.png
159	top-3 deltas addressed:
160	  1. palette: removed 2 redundant hues, kept 1 signal + 2 neutrals
161	  2. typography: panel letters 8pt -> 9pt bold, axis labels 6.5 -> 7pt
162	  3. hero panel area: 30% -> 42% via gridspec width_ratios
163	iterations: 2
164	remaining gaps (acknowledged): exemplar uses subtle background tint
165	  in hero panel; we did not replicate (manuscript palette has no
166	  near-white tint family available)
167	```
168	
169	## Provenance
170	
171	Distilled from SkillTest R6.A user feedback. The R1-R6 figure rounds
172	established that rule-based skills (figure-layout-defaults,
173	academic-plotting) plateau at "correct / submittable" but do not reach
174	"beautiful." The aesthetic-exemplar paradigm — REFERENCE LIBRARY +
175	DIFF WORKFLOW — is the SkillTest hypothesis for closing that gap.
176	
177	R-future validation: re-run R5.C 7-panel or R6.A 9-panel through this
178	workflow + user visual review. If the user grades the result
179	materially better than rule-only candidate, this paradigm is
180	load-bearing. If not, the gap requires a vision-capable iterative
181	loop or a human designer.
182

file.content:
---
slug: figure-aesthetic-exemplars
summary: Reference-driven figure design — diff your figure against the closest exemplar in the gallery, identify 3 biggest deltas, revise. Different paradigm from rule-based figure-layout-defaults / academic-plotting.
layer: logical
tools:
version: 1
status: draft-from-SkillTest-R-future
supersedes:
references: figure-layout-defaults, academic-plotting
provenance: SkillTest-R6.A-aesthetic-ceiling-finding
---

# Skill — Figure Aesthetic Exemplars (DRAFT — SkillTest research zone)

> This is a draft from SkillTest, not a live MinionsOS skill. Authored
> in response to R6.A user feedback: "the skill's palette is maybe slightly
> more disciplined, but it's nowhere near beautiful or good-looking."

## What this skill is

A REFERENCE LIBRARY plus a JUDGEMENT WORKFLOW, not a rule list. The
companion skills [[figure-layout-defaults]] and [[academic-plotting]]
make figures *correct* (no rasterised text, no clashing hues, no empty
quadrants). This skill makes figures *beautiful* — closer to what
appears in a recent Nature or Cell publication.

The premise: rules can constrain (don't do X) but cannot generate
(produce Y). Beauty in scientific figures comes from specific,
non-rule-encodable choices: a particular palette ratio, a particular
typographic rhythm, a particular allocation of white space. The only
way to learn these is to STUDY EXEMPLARS — figures that are
empirically beautiful — and DIFF YOUR FIGURE AGAINST THEM.

## When to invoke

- You have a draft figure that satisfies all rule-based skills
  (figure-layout-defaults, academic-plotting) but the user / co-author
  has flagged it as "looks fine but not beautiful," "lacks any spark,"
  or "nowhere near beautiful or good-looking."
- You are preparing a Nature / Cell / Science cover-eligible figure
  where aesthetic quality matters beyond submittability.
- You have iteration budget: this skill expects 2-4 render-revise cycles,
  not single-pass rule application.

Do NOT invoke if:
- The figure is pre-submission and only needs to be SUBMITTABLE (use
  rule-based skills only).
- You have no iteration budget — exemplar diffing assumes you can
  re-render after revision.

## Procedure

### Step 1 — Render your figure with rule-based skills first

Apply [[figure-layout-defaults]] and [[academic-plotting]] FIRST.
Render the figure. Save the artefact at, say, `figures/draft1.png`.
Don't skip this — exemplar workflow assumes a baseline-correct figure
to refine.

### Step 2 — Pick the closest exemplar from the gallery

Open `gallery/index.md`. Find the exemplar that matches your figure's
ARCHETYPE most closely:

| Archetype | Exemplar |
|---|---|
| Multi-panel hero with dose-response + safety + biomarker | `fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich` |
| Heatmap-heavy single-cell / dimension reduction | `fig4-single-cell-systems-rich` |
| Single-panel grouped bar (ablation, comparison) | `bars_ablation_Cancer` |
| Multi-bar cross-category comparison | `correctness_by_category` |
| Multi-method sweep with parameter ranges | `results_sweep` |

If your figure type isn't in the gallery, pick the *closest* archetype
and note the gap. Future R rounds extend the gallery.

### Step 3 — Open the exemplar's annotation card

Each exemplar in `gallery/` has a sibling `<exemplar>.annotation.md`
file documenting:

- **Extracted palette**: actual hex values dominant in the rendered
  figure (not generic rules, but what THIS exemplar uses)
- **Palette ratio**: percentage of pixels in each colour family
  (background grey vs signal accent vs subordinate hue)
- **Typography**: font sizes per element class (axis label, panel
  letter, caption note, in-panel annotation)
- **White space allocation**: ratio of panel area to whitespace
- **Visual rhythm**: where the eye lands first, second, third
- **What makes this figure work**: 3-5 bullet points naming the
  specific design choices that differentiate it from a "correct but
  bland" figure

### Step 4 — Diff your figure against the exemplar

Open both side-by-side (Playwright HTML page or local image viewer).
Identify the 3 biggest visual deltas. Examples:

- Your palette has 4 saturated hues; exemplar has 1 signal + 2 neutrals
  + heavy background grey.
- Your panel labels are 7-pt bold; exemplar uses 9-pt bold with extra
  weight contrast.
- Your hero panel fills 30% of canvas; exemplar's hero fills 45%.
- Your bars use 0-100 axis with 35% empty top; exemplar zooms to data
  range with 10% headroom.

Rank the 3 deltas by aesthetic impact (typography is usually high
impact; precise hex value is usually low).

### Step 5 — Revise the script to address top-3 deltas

DON'T re-do the figure from scratch. Edit the existing script to
address only the 3 named deltas. Re-render to `draft2.png`.

### Step 6 — Re-diff and decide

Compare draft2 vs the exemplar again. If the gap has closed
substantially, stop. If 1-2 of the 3 deltas remain, do one more
iteration. **Cap at 4 iterations** — beyond that, you're polishing
what should have been redesigned.

## What this skill does NOT do

- It does NOT rewrite [[figure-layout-defaults]] or [[academic-plotting]].
  Use those first; this skill is the polish step.
- It does NOT generate exemplars. The gallery is curated from real
  published figures.
- It does NOT replace human aesthetic judgment for a venue cover. For
  Nature cover-grade figures, a human designer still adds ~20% beyond
  what this workflow produces.
- It does NOT tell you a specific colour to use. It tells you ratios
  and exemplar precedents; the runner picks specific hex values
  matching the manuscript palette family.

## Pitfalls

- **Picking the wrong exemplar.** If your figure is a single-panel
  bar chart and you diff against `fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich` (a
  4-panel hero composite), the deltas will be misleading. Match
  archetype first.
- **Copying the exemplar's palette pixel-perfect.** The point is to
  learn the RATIO and the FAMILY, not to literally copy hex values
  from a different paper's figure. Translate the lesson to your
  manuscript's palette.
- **Endless iteration.** 4-iteration cap is a hard limit. If you can't
  close the gap in 4 cycles, the figure needs structural redesign,
  not aesthetic polish.
- **Ignoring the rule-based baseline.** This skill ASSUMES rules are
  already applied. If you skip [[figure-layout-defaults]] first, the
  exemplar diff becomes "your figure is wrong AND ugly" — fix wrong
  first.

## Output habit

When delivering a polished figure, document:

```
# aesthetic exemplar workflow
exemplar_used: gallery/fig3-in-vivo-efficacy-rich.png
top-3 deltas addressed:
  1. palette: removed 2 redundant hues, kept 1 signal + 2 neutrals
  2. typography: panel letters 8pt -> 9pt bold, axis labels 6.5 -> 7pt
  3. hero panel area: 30% -> 42% via gridspec width_ratios
iterations: 2
remaining gaps (acknowledged): exemplar uses subtle background tint
  in hero panel; we did not replicate (manuscript palette has no
  near-white tint family available)
```

## Provenance

Distilled from SkillTest R6.A user feedback. The R1-R6 figure rounds
established that rule-based skills (figure-layout-defaults,
academic-plotting) plateau at "correct / submittable" but do not reach
"beautiful." The aesthetic-exemplar paradigm — REFERENCE LIBRARY +
DIFF WORKFLOW — is the SkillTest hypothesis for closing that gap.

R-future validation: re-run R5.C 7-panel or R6.A 9-panel through this
workflow + user visual review. If the user grades the result
materially better than rule-only candidate, this paradigm is
load-bearing. If not, the gap requires a vision-capable iterative
loop or a human designer.

input.content:
"""Volcano plot — data.json (3000 genes), thresholds from data."""
import json
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patheffects as pe

# Font stack: Arial → Helvetica → DejaVu Sans → Liberation Sans
# Do NOT override per element — all text inherits from here.
mpl.rcParams.update({
    "font.family": "sans-serif",
    "font.sans-serif": ["Arial", "Helvetica", "DejaVu Sans", "Liberation Sans"],
    "svg.fonttype": "none",
    "pdf.fonttype": 42,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.linewidth": 0.8,
    "legend.frameon": False,
    "font.size": 9,
})

PALETTE = {
    "up":      "#C0392B",   # red — upregulated
    "down":    "#2471A3",   # blue — downregulated
    "ns":      "#AAAAAA",   # grey — non-significant
    "thresh":  "#555555",   # dashed threshold lines
    "annot":   "#1A1A1A",   # annotation text
}

# ── load data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
with open("data.json") as f:
    d = json.load(f)

x = np.array(d["log2fc"])
y = np.array(d["neg_log10_p"])
fc_thresh  = d["thresholds"]["abs_log2fc"]      # 1.0
p_thresh   = d["thresholds"]["neg_log10_p"]     # 2.0

# ── classify ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
sig = y >= p_thresh
up   = sig & (x >  fc_thresh)
down = sig & (x < -fc_thresh)
ns   = ~(up | down)

# ── top-5 by neg_log10_p (among significant) ───────────────────────────────
sig_idx = np.where(sig)[0]
top5_idx = sig_idx[np.argsort(y[sig_idx])[::-1][:5]]

# ── figure ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))

# scatter layers: ns first, then coloured on top
ax.scatter(x[ns],   y[ns],   s=5,  c=PALETTE["ns"],   alpha=0.45, linewidths=0, rasterized=True, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(x[down], y[down], s=7,  c=PALETTE["down"],  alpha=0.75, linewidths=0, rasterized=True, zorder=2)
ax.scatter(x[up],   y[up],   s=7,  c=PALETTE["up"],    alpha=0.75, linewidths=0, rasterized=True, zorder=2)

# threshold lines
ax.axhline(p_thresh,  color=PALETTE["thresh"], lw=0.8, ls="--", zorder=0)
ax.axvline( fc_thresh, color=PALETTE["thresh"], lw=0.8, ls="--", zorder=0)
ax.axvline(-fc_thresh, color=PALETTE["thresh"], lw=0.8, ls="--", zorder=0)

# annotate top-5
for i in top5_idx:
    label = f"G{i}"
    ax.annotate(
        label,
        xy=(x[i], y[i]),
        xytext=(4, 3),
        textcoords="offset points",
        fontsize=7,
        color=PALETTE["annot"],
        path_effects=[pe.withStroke(linewidth=1.8, foreground="white")],
        zorder=5,
    )

# counts in corners
n_up   = int(up.sum())
n_down = int(down.sum())
ax.text(0.98, 0.97, f"Up: {n_up}",   transform=ax.transAxes,
        ha="right", va="top", fontsize=8, color=PALETTE["up"])
ax.text(0.02, 0.97, f"Down: {n_down}", transform=ax.transAxes,
        ha="left",  va="top", fontsize=8, color=PALETTE["down"])

# axes
ax.set_xlabel("log$_2$ fold change", fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("$-$log$_{10}$(p)", fontsize=9)
ax.tick_params(direction="out", length=2.5, width=0.6, labelsize=8)

# legend
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
handles = [
    Line2D([0], [0], marker="o", color="w", markerfacecolor=PALETTE["up"],   markersize=6, label=f"Up (FC>{fc_thresh}, p<{10**-p_thresh:.3f})"),
    Line2D([0], [0], marker="o", color="w", markerfacecolor=PALETTE["down"], markersize=6, label=f"Down (FC<-{fc_thresh}, p<{10**-p_thresh:.3f})"),
    Line2D([0], [0], marker="o", color="w", markerfacecolor=PALETTE["ns"],   markersize=6, label="Non-significant"),
]
ax.legend(handles=handles, fontsize=7.5, loc="upper center",
          bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.13), ncol=3, handletextpad=0.3, columnspacing=0.8)

fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.05, 1, 1])

fig.savefig("figure.pdf", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.svg", bbox_inches="tight")
print(f"Saved figure.pdf / figure.png / figure.svg  |  up={n_up}  down={n_down}  ns={int(ns.sum())}")

"""Volcano plot — data.json (3000 genes), thresholds from data."""
import json
import numpy as np
import matplotlib as mpl
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patheffects as pe

# Font stack: Arial → Helvetica → DejaVu Sans → Liberation Sans
# Do NOT override per element — all text inherits from here.
mpl.rcParams.update({
    "font.family": "sans-serif",
    "font.sans-serif": ["Arial", "Helvetica", "DejaVu Sans", "Liberation Sans"],
    "svg.fonttype": "none",
    "pdf.fonttype": 42,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.linewidth": 0.8,
    "legend.frameon": False,
    "font.size": 9,
})

PALETTE = {
    "up":      "#C0392B",   # red — upregulated
    "down":    "#2471A3",   # blue — downregulated
    "ns":      "#AAAAAA",   # grey — non-significant
    "thresh":  "#555555",   # dashed threshold lines
    "annot":   "#1A1A1A",   # annotation text
}

# ── load data ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
with open("data.json") as f:
    d = json.load(f)

x = np.array(d["log2fc"])
y = np.array(d["neg_log10_p"])
fc_thresh  = d["thresholds"]["abs_log2fc"]      # 1.0
p_thresh   = d["thresholds"]["neg_log10_p"]     # 2.0

# ── classify ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
sig = y >= p_thresh
up   = sig & (x >  fc_thresh)
down = sig & (x < -fc_thresh)
ns   = ~(up | down)

# ── top-5 by neg_log10_p (among significant) ───────────────────────────────
sig_idx = np.where(sig)[0]
top5_idx = sig_idx[np.argsort(y[sig_idx])[::-1][:5]]

# ── figure ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 5))

# scatter layers: ns first, then coloured on top
ax.scatter(x[ns],   y[ns],   s=5,  c=PALETTE["ns"],   alpha=0.45, linewidths=0, rasterized=True, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(x[down], y[down], s=7,  c=PALETTE["down"],  alpha=0.75, linewidths=0, rasterized=True, zorder=2)
ax.scatter(x[up],   y[up],   s=7,  c=PALETTE["up"],    alpha=0.75, linewidths=0, rasterized=True, zorder=2)

# threshold lines
ax.axhline(p_thresh,  color=PALETTE["thresh"], lw=0.8, ls="--", zorder=0)
ax.axvline( fc_thresh, color=PALETTE["thresh"], lw=0.8, ls="--", zorder=0)
ax.axvline(-fc_thresh, color=PALETTE["thresh"], lw=0.8, ls="--", zorder=0)

# annotate top-5
for i in top5_idx:
    label = f"G{i}"
    ax.annotate(
        label,
        xy=(x[i], y[i]),
        xytext=(4, 3),
        textcoords="offset points",
        fontsize=7,
        color=PALETTE["annot"],
        path_effects=[pe.withStroke(linewidth=1.8, foreground="white")],
        zorder=5,
    )

# counts in corners
n_up   = int(up.sum())
n_down = int(down.sum())
ax.text(0.98, 0.97, f"Up: {n_up}",   transform=ax.transAxes,
        ha="right", va="top", fontsize=8, color=PALETTE["up"])
ax.text(0.02, 0.97, f"Down: {n_down}", transform=ax.transAxes,
        ha="left",  va="top", fontsize=8, color=PALETTE["down"])

# axes
ax.set_xlabel("log$_2$ fold change", fontsize=9)
ax.set_ylabel("$-$log$_{10}$(p)", fontsize=9)
ax.tick_params(direction="out", length=2.5, width=0.6, labelsize=8)

# legend
from matplotlib.lines import Line2D
handles = [
    Line2D([0], [0], marker="o", color="w", markerfacecolor=PALETTE["up"],   markersize=6, label=f"Up (FC>{fc_thresh}, p<{10**-p_thresh:.3f})"),
    Line2D([0], [0], marker="o", color="w", markerfacecolor=PALETTE["down"], markersize=6, label=f"Down (FC<-{fc_thresh}, p<{10**-p_thresh:.3f})"),
    Line2D([0], [0], marker="o", color="w", markerfacecolor=PALETTE["ns"],   markersize=6, label="Non-significant"),
]
ax.legend(handles=handles, fontsize=7.5, loc="upper center",
          bbox_to_anchor=(0.5, -0.13), ncol=3, handletextpad=0.3, columnspacing=0.8)

fig.tight_layout(rect=[0, 0.05, 1, 1])

fig.savefig("figure.pdf", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.png", dpi=300, bbox_inches="tight")
fig.savefig("figure.svg", bbox_inches="tight")
print(f"Saved figure.pdf / figure.png / figure.svg  |  up={n_up}  down={n_down}  ns={int(ns.sum())}")

All four required files are in place:

- `figure.pdf` (154 KB) — vector, TrueType fonts embedded
- `figure.png` (221 KB, 300 dpi)
- `figure.svg` (159 KB, editable text)
- `gen_figure.py` — reproducible generation script
- `caption.tex` — LaTeX `\caption{...}` ready to drop into a `figure` environment

The plot shows 3,000 genes with up (red, n=235) / down (blue, n=230) / non-significant (grey, n=2,535) coloring at |log₂FC|=1 and p=0.01 thresholds, with the top-5 most significant genes labelled.
